name: Vía de la Plata: Cáceres Historic Center Loop
name_original: Recorrido urbano de Cáceres — circular por el casco histórico
description: Jogging the 3.5-mile Vía de la Plata: Cáceres Historic Center Loop offers a moderate route through UNESCO-listed cobbled streets.
region_intro: Jogging around Cáceres offers a diverse range of routes through both historical urban settings and protected natural landscapes. The region features extensive natural spaces like Los Llanos de Cáceres-Sierra de Fuentes, known for their ecological importance. Joggers can explore unique granite formations and ponds, as well as various urban parks within the city itself. This blend provides varied terrain suitable for different running preferences. Best jogging routes around Cáceres The most popular jogging route is Vía de la Plata: Cáceres Historic Center Loop, a 3.5 miles (5.6 km) trail that takes about 36 minutes to complete. This route allows runners to explore the UNESCO-listed cobbled streets of the historic center. Another top favourite among local runners is Mining and Hermitages Route — circular from Cáceres, a moderate 6.1 miles (9.8 km) path. This trail combines natural landscapes with historical elements, offering a varied running experience. Local runners also love the Portanchito and Sanctuary of the Mountain Trail, a 5.1 miles (8.2 km) trail leading through rewarding climbs and scenic highlights, often completed in about 57 minutes. Jogging around Cáceres is defined by historic urban areas, protected natural spaces, and unique granite formations. The network offers options for various ability levels, from easy urban loops to more challenging trails. The routes in Cáceres are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 3.9 stars from over 10 reviews. More than 1000 runners have used komoot to explore Cáceres's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Cáceres?
A: Cáceres offers a wide variety of running options, with over 120 routes available for exploration. These routes cater to different preferences, ranging from urban jogs to challenging trails through natural landscapes.

Q: Are there easy running routes suitable for beginners in Cáceres?
A: Yes, Cáceres has options for beginners. There are 2 routes specifically categorized as easy. Within the city, green spaces like Parque del Príncipe provide accessible and gentle paths for a relaxed jog. For a slightly longer, moderate option, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1345914672/via-de-la-plata-caceres-historic-center-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía de la Plata: Cáceres Historic Center Loop&lt;/a&gt; offers a unique urban experience.

Q: What are some challenging running trails around Cáceres?
A: For runners seeking a challenge, Cáceres offers 33 difficult routes. A notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1345586731/portanchito-and-sanctuary-of-the-mountain-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Portanchito and Sanctuary of the Mountain Trail&lt;/a&gt;, an 8.2 km (5.1-mile) route featuring rewarding climbs and scenic views of the historic center. Another demanding trail is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1345637822/paseo-alto-y-la-sierrilla-circular-desde-el-estadio-principe-felipe" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Paseo Alto y la Sierrilla — circular desde el Estadio Príncipe Felipe&lt;/a&gt;, which also includes significant elevation changes.

Q: Can I find circular running routes in Cáceres?
A: Absolutely. Many of the running routes in Cáceres are circular, offering convenient starting and ending points. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1343774062/mining-and-hermitages-route-circular-from-caceres" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mining and Hermitages Route — circular from Cáceres&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 9.8 km (6.1-mile) loop that combines natural landscapes with historical elements. Another great circular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1345655929/parque-del-principe-y-olivar-de-los-frailes-circular-por-el-cerro-otero" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parque del Príncipe y Olivar de los Frailes — circular por el Cerro Otero&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What kind of natural landscapes can I expect to see while jogging in Cáceres?
A: Cáceres and its surroundings boast diverse natural landscapes. You can run through protected natural spaces like Los Llanos de Cáceres-Sierra de Fuentes, known for their ecological importance. The region also features unique granite formations and ponds, particularly around the Monumento Natural de Los Barruecos. Urban parks within the city provide green oases for a refreshing run.

Q: What are some interesting landmarks or attractions I can see along the running routes?
A: Many running routes in Cáceres offer a chance to see historical and natural attractions. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1345914672/via-de-la-plata-caceres-historic-center-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía de la Plata: Cáceres Historic Center Loop&lt;/a&gt; takes you through the UNESCO-listed cobbled streets, passing by sights like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/780383" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cáceres Main Square&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3783241" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Co-Cathedral of Santa María&lt;/a&gt;. Outside the city, areas like Los Barruecos feature unique granite formations, including the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5228003" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Barruecos – Game of Thrones Filming Location&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other runners say about their experience jogging in Cáceres?
A: The running routes in Cáceres are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 3.9 stars from over 10 reviews. Runners often praise the unique blend of historic urban settings and the tranquility of the natural landscapes, highlighting the variety of terrain and the well-maintained paths.

Q: Are there any specific considerations for jogging in Cáceres during summer?
A: Cáceres can experience significant heat during the summer months. It's advisable to plan your runs for early mornings or late evenings to avoid the hottest parts of the day. Staying hydrated is crucial, and choosing routes with some shade, such as those through urban parks or tree-lined paths, can make your summer jogging more comfortable.

Q: Are there urban running routes in Cáceres?
A: Yes, Cáceres offers several urban running routes. Beyond the historic center, green spaces like Parque del Príncipe, Olivar Chico de los Frailes, and Parque del Perú provide accessible paths within the city. These parks offer a pleasant environment for a city jog, often with amenities nearby.

Q: Are there any long-distance running options in Cáceres?
A: While the guide focuses on routes up to about 10 km, the extensive network of trails in and around Cáceres, including those in protected natural spaces, allows for longer runs by combining multiple segments or exploring the broader areas of Los Llanos de Cáceres-Sierra de Fuentes and Sierra de San Pedro. For a challenging longer option, the Pantano del Casar Loop Trail, though not in the immediate guide, is a 16.1 km (10.0-mile) difficult trail around a reservoir.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the running trails in Cáceres?
A: Generally, dogs are welcome on many natural trails and in some urban parks in Cáceres, especially if kept on a leash. However, it's always best to check local regulations for specific parks or protected areas, as rules can vary. Always ensure your dog is well-behaved and that you clean up after them.
tips:
This palace combines two styles: Renaissance and Gothic.

The name is derived from its owner in the 18th century, Dª Josefa de Ovando, wife of General D. Antonio Vicente de Arce.

Like many other strong houses and palaces in Cáceres Monumental, it was built in the 19th century. XIV, being renovated in the 15th and 16th centuries.

It has a tower that is topless and on the doorway a semi-cylindrical machicolation accompanied by shields of the Ovando-Mogollón family.

It has had many and diverse uses, headquarters of the Extremadura Newspaper, Faculty of Law and currently Central Services of the University of Extremadura.

Walking anywhere along its narrow cobblestone streets is like traveling back in time. You'll find yourself surrounded by mansions, Renaissance palaces, and churches crowned with stork nests, and you'll understand why Cáceres is a World Heritage City. Get your camera ready to photograph one of Spain's most beautiful historic centers.

Located on Amargura Street, it was erected between the 15th and 16th centuries. It is a building that has Gothic and Renaissance elements.
Its main façade follows the typology of the Cáceres palaces, made of granite ashlar, highlighting the semicircular arch doorway, with large voussoirs that reach to the ground, the large Carvajal shield, framed in an alfiz, and a balcony of pointed arch corner.

The Casa Palacio de los Becerra is a Gothic-style building located in the Plaza de San Jorge inside the monumental complex of the city. It is currently the headquarters of the Mercedes Calles and Carlos Ballestero Foundation.

faqs.question: Where does the Vía de la Plata: Cáceres Historic Center Loop start?
faqs.answer: The loop primarily navigates through the historic center of Cáceres. While there isn't one single designated trailhead for this specific urban loop, a good starting point could be near the Historic Center of Cáceres itself, perhaps close to the Plaza Mayor, which is easily accessible and offers parking options nearby.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this jogging route?
faqs.answer: Within the Historic Center of Cáceres, the terrain consists mainly of cobbled streets and stone pathways, which can be uneven and require careful footing. As the route might extend slightly beyond the immediate old town, you could encounter paved tracks, and potentially some dirt or gravel sections.

faqs.question: Is the Vía de la Plata: Cáceres Historic Center Loop suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route is considered moderate in difficulty. While the overall elevation changes are minimal, the uneven cobbled surfaces within the historic center can be more demanding than smooth pavement. Beginners should be mindful of their footing, but the relatively short distance makes it accessible.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: This route offers a rich historical and architectural experience. You'll pass by landmarks such as the Palacio de la Generala, the Carvajal Palace, and the Becerra Manor House. The entire Historic Center of Cáceres, a UNESCO World Heritage site, is a highlight in itself, showcasing Roman, Moorish, Gothic, and Renaissance styles.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog this loop?
faqs.answer: The best times to jog this route are typically spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, the weather in Extremadura is generally milder and more pleasant, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the cooler, potentially wetter days of winter. Early mornings or late afternoons are also recommended to avoid crowds and the midday sun.

faqs.question: Is this jogging route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, the historic center of Cáceres is generally dog-friendly, but dogs should always be kept on a leash, especially in crowded areas and near historical monuments. Be prepared to clean up after your pet. Note that the cobbled streets might be challenging for some dogs' paws, especially during hot weather.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to jog this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no permits or entrance fees required to jog along the Vía de la Plata through the historic center of Cáceres. It is a public route through the city.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or drink near the trail?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. The historic center of Cáceres, particularly around the Plaza Mayor, is bustling with cafes, restaurants, and bars where you can grab a drink or a meal before or after your jog. You'll find plenty of options for traditional Extremaduran cuisine and refreshing beverages.

faqs.question: What makes jogging this specific route unique?
faqs.answer: Jogging this route offers a unique blend of physical activity and deep historical immersion. You're tracing a path along one of Spain's oldest Roman roads within a UNESCO World Heritage city, surrounded by remarkably preserved architecture from various eras. It's a chance to experience centuries of history and culture while enjoying an urban jog.

faqs.question: How can I get to the historic center of Cáceres using public transportation?
faqs.answer: Cáceres has a local bus network that serves the city. Several bus lines have stops within walking distance of the historic center, making it convenient to access. Check the local bus schedules and routes for the most up-to-date information on reaching the old town.

faqs.question: What should I wear when jogging in Cáceres, especially in summer?
faqs.answer: For jogging in Cáceres, especially during summer, it's crucial to wear lightweight, breathable athletic clothing. Opt for moisture-wicking fabrics. A hat, sunglasses, and plenty of sunscreen are essential due to the strong sun. Always carry water to stay hydrated. In cooler months, layers are advisable.
